critic_raw:
The use of a stratified random split on time-series sensor data is the central flaw. Equipment sensors produce strongly autocorrelated signals — future windows from the same machine will appear in training while earlier windows are in test (or vice versa), allowing the model to learn machine-specific baseline states from 'future' observations and apply them to 'past' predictions. This is not a theoretical concern: for anomaly detection specifically, the model learns what normal looks like for each machine, and if normal segments from the same machine span splits, the learned normal boundary reflects post-failure machine states, inflating apparent performance.

The CNN-for-spatial-hierarchies framing is a category error. CNNs exploit spatial locality in images; for 1D time-series they function as local temporal filters, which is a defensible choice but not because of 'spatial hierarchies.' No ablation against a simpler 1D convolution or dilated causal convolution baseline is proposed — the architectural claim is untestable.

The threshold-based baseline is underspecified: how were thresholds set? If thresholds were tuned on any data that overlaps with or follows the training period, the baseline evaluation is not fair. The design says it was 'tuned to match the complexity permitted for each choice' — this is vague enough to permit test-contaminated baseline tuning.

The primary metric is reduction in false positive rate, but this is only meaningful at a fixed threshold or across a specific operating regime. FPR without a corresponding TPR or precision value is not a complete performance characterization — a model that never fires achieves FPR=0 while being useless. Lead time to detection is mentioned as a secondary metric but is the operationally critical measure for predictive maintenance and should be primary.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on temporal sensor data allows future machine-state observations from the same equipment to appear in training, creating temporal leakage that inflates anomaly detection performance because the model learns what 'normal' looks like from data recorded after the events it is supposedly predicting.

The baseline threshold-based system lacks a clear tuning protocol — if thresholds were set using data that temporally overlaps with or follows the training window, the comparison is not fair to the baseline.

False positive rate as the primary metric is only meaningful at a fixed operating point; without specifying the corresponding TPR or recall constraint, the metric is incomplete and allows the model to trivially 'win' by being overly conservative.

The claim that CNNs are preferred for 'spatial hierarchies in time-series data' is a category error — CNNs on 1D series act as local temporal filters, not spatial feature extractors — and no ablation is proposed to verify whether the specific CNN architecture provides gains over simpler alternatives.
Detection lead time — the operationally critical metric for predictive maintenance — is listed only as a secondary metric despite being the primary business objective of the experiment.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on temporal sensor data allows future machine-state observations from the same equipment to appear in training, creating temporal leakage that inflates anomaly detection performance because the model learns what 'normal' looks like from data recorded after the events it is supposedly predicting.

The baseline threshold-based system lacks a clear tuning protocol — if thresholds were set using data that temporally overlaps with or follows the training window, the comparison is not fair to the baseline.

False positive rate as the primary metric is only meaningful at a fixed operating point; without specifying the corresponding TPR or recall constraint, the metric is incomplete and allows the model to trivially 'win' by being overly conservative.

The claim that CNNs are preferred for 'spatial hierarchies in time-series data' is a category error — CNNs on 1D series act as local temporal filters, not spatial feature extractors — and no ablation is proposed to verify whether the specific CNN architecture provides gains over simpler alternatives.
Detection lead time — the operationally critical metric for predictive maintenance — is listed only as a secondary metric despite being the primary business objective of the experiment.
